title: Using the drawknife on chair backs and spindles
subtitle: How to shape curved chair-back spindles and bowed back rails with the drawknife
excerpt: Chair-back spindles and bow rails require taper, gentle curvature, and consistent section. This technique tutorial covers the shaving-horse setup, the cut direction, working with and against the grain in green ash and oak, and the transitions from drawknife to spokeshave.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the chair-back spindle shaping method; Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry for the drawknife-to-spokeshave transition.

glossaryTerms.definition: A two-handled blade pulled toward the body along the workpiece. The green-woodwork rough-shaping tool.
A foot-pedalled bench that clamps the workpiece for two-handed drawknife and spokeshave work.
A small two-handled plane for shaping curves. Flat-sole and round-sole variants exist.
Freshly felled or recently riven wood, still high in moisture content. Green ash and oak shape most readily at the shaving horse.
Split along the grain with a froe and maul. Riven spindle blanks have straight grain and are stronger than sawn ones.

content.text: Chair-back spindles are long thin pieces that carry the horizontal stress of a back rail and the person leaning against it. A
riven
spindle made from
green
ash or oak and shaped at the
shaving horse
has grain running continuously from end to end. A sawn spindle can have short grain at a bend, which will snap under load. The
drawknife
roughes the shape; the
spokeshave
refines it.
When to use this technique

Use the drawknife on riven green ash for Windsor chair spindles (tapered cylinders, 9 mm at the top tenon and 16 mm at the seat tenon, 350 to 400 mm long). Use it also for the bow rail of a Windsor chair back, and for the shaved chair leg between the foot and the seat socket.
What you need

The shaving horse is essential: it clamps the workpiece and frees both hands for the drawknife. Without it the work shifts on every pull stroke. Green ash is the recommended species for spindles; it bends under steam and is strong in the long-grain direction. Green oak for the bow rail; it is stiffer and holds the rail shape as it dries. The drawknife edge must be sharp enough to pare an end-grain shaving without tearing; test and strop before starting.
Shaving-horse setup and grip

Sit on the shaving horse with both legs extended and feet on the foot rest. Press the foot rest down to clamp the workpiece under the dumbhead. The dumbhead pressure should hold the workpiece firmly against the shaving horse beam with no sliding or rocking. If the workpiece slips, add a strip of rough cloth under the dumbhead.

Hold the drawknife with both hands, thumbs on the blade back, fingers wrapped over the handles. The bevel faces up for the roughing cut: bevel-up gives a shallower, more controllable cut angle. Pull the blade toward your body along the length of the workpiece; the blade should ride just under the surface, lifting a continuous shaving.
Grain direction on chair spindles

The grain in a riven ash spindle runs diagonally from one end to the other. Half the length will have grain running with the drawknife pull; the other half will run against it. At the midpoint of the spindle there will be a transition zone. Work from each end toward the middle on the first roughing pass. Take long passes with the cut going with the grain first; then come back from the other end. Where the two passes meet, use a lighter cut and be ready for the grain to reverse at any point.
The drawknife-to-spokeshave transition

The drawknife leaves facets; the spokeshave removes them. Switch to the flat-bottomed spokeshave when the spindle is within 2 mm of the target diameter. For a taper spindle, the spokeshave is used with the tool angled slightly toward the narrowing end so the cut takes more from the thick side. The round-bottomed spokeshave follows concave curves such as the underside of a bow rail.
What a successful cut looks like

A clean drawknife cut on green ash lifts a long, curling shaving that holds its curl after it leaves the blade. The cut surface shows fine parallel lines from the blade edge. A torn surface means the cut went against the grain; a burnished surface means the edge is dull. Strop every fifteen minutes.
Common mistakes
Working the full length of the spindle in one pull: the grain direction changes along the length and a long single-pass cut will tear at the midpoint. Work each half toward the centre.

Using the bevel-down position for the roughing pass: bevel-down makes the cut angle steeper and the drawknife digs rather than slices. Bevel-up for roughing; switch to bevel-down only for paring end-grain sections.
Not releasing foot pressure between cuts: lift your foot off the pedal when repositioning the drawknife or checking the work. The clamping stays on until the foot lifts.

Shaping a spindle for a steamed-bend chair bow in seasoned wood: the bending step requires green wood. Shaping can be done in green wood; the bend must be done while it is still green and then dried in the bent position.
